The dev middleware (`apps/dashboard/src/middleware.ts`) does the inverse rewrite — a request with hostname `lvh.me` in dev is redirected to `localhost` so the Clerk session cookie is honored. Production uses the real dashboard hostname; the rewrite is dev-only.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `apps/dashboard/src/middleware.ts` redirects `lvh.me` → `localhost` in dev.
Step 2: Confirm the checkout / portal handlers rewrite `localhost` → `lvh.me` before calling Core.
Step 3: In dev, set `CORE_API_URL=http://api.lvh.me:9999` (or similar) and confirm the handlers' outbound URL uses the lvh.me host.

acceptance_check.title: 15 integration types are surfaced as connection cards in the dashboard's integrations page
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/integrations/domain/types.ts` enumerates 15 integration types: Slack, GitHub, Jira, AWS CloudWatch, HubSpot, Trello, PostgreSQL, Linear, Sentry, MongoDB, Datadog, PagerDuty, Grafana, Confluence, Webhooks. The integration catalog page renders one connection card per type with the Composio logo, the integration description, and a "Connect" CTA. Each integration has a type-specific Zod schema in `apps/dashboard/src/lib/api/schemas.ts`; credentials are forwarded to the Core API for KMS-envelope encryption and DynamoDB storage.

steps.step: Step 1: Sign in as admin, load `/dashboard/integrations`, and confirm 15 integration cards are rendered (Slack, GitHub, Jira, AWS CloudWatch, HubSpot, Trello, PostgreSQL, Linear, Sentry, MongoDB, Datadog, PagerDuty, Grafana, Confluence, Webhooks).
Step 2: Confirm `Integration` type carries a `composioTriggerId` field and the integration-domain types file lists exactly 15 type identifiers.
Step 3: Confirm the dashboard's `next.config.mjs` `images.remotePatterns` allow-lists `logos.composio.dev` and `backend.composio.dev`.

acceptance_check.title: 9 bounded contexts (investigation, approvals, audit, identity, onboarding, team, integrations, billing, settings) + shared with DDD layers
acceptance_check.description: The dashboard's `src/contexts/` directory contains 10 bounded contexts: investigation, approvals, audit, identity, onboarding, team, integrations, billing, settings, shared. Each context has a DDD layout with only the layers it needs (domain, application, infrastructure, presentation, api). The website's `src/contexts/` directory contains 3 contexts: marketing, legal, shell. `index.ts` barrel files do not exist in any context in either app — all imports use direct deep paths. Per-context i18n files live at `infrastructure/i18n/{en,pt-br}.json` and are deep-merged by `apps/{website,dashboard}/src/lib/i18n/compose.ts`.

steps.step: Step 1: `ls apps/dashboard/src/contexts/` shows investigation, approvals, audit, identity, onboarding, team, integrations, billing, settings, shared (10 contexts).
Step 2: `ls apps/website/src/contexts/` shows marketing, legal, shell (3 contexts).
Step 3: Confirm no `index.ts` exists in any of those context directories.

acceptance_check.title: withAuth: 401/403/429 enforcement at the API boundary
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/src/lib/api/with-auth.ts#withAuth(handler, options)` enforces, in order: (1) Clerk auth — no session returns 401 with `{ error: 'Authentication required.' }`; (2) tenant validation — no `orgId` returns 403 with `{ error: 'Profile setup required. Please complete onboarding.' }` (skippable with `allowNoOrg: true`); (3) role guard — `adminOnly: true` or `requiredRole: 'admin'` returns 403 with `{ error: 'Admin access required.' }` / `{ error: 'Insufficient role for this action.' }`; (4) per-user rate limit — 60 req/min default, 429 with `Retry-After` header on excess; (5) staff-only — `staffOnly: true` returns 403 for non-staff emails. The wrapped handler runs only after all guards pass.

steps.step: Step 1: With no session, `GET /api/settings` returns 401 with the documented body.
Step 2: With a session but no `orgId`, `GET /api/settings` returns 403 with the documented body.
Step 3: With a member session, `POST /api/billing/checkout` (adminOnly) returns 403; with 70 requests in 60s the 70th returns 429 with a `Retry-After` header.

acceptance_check.title: BFF pattern: Clerk session JWT forwarded as Bearer to Core API; never exposed to the browser
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/src/lib/api/get-backend-token.ts` reads the Clerk session JWT server-side via `getToken()` from `@clerk/nextjs/server`. The dashboard's `http-api-client.ts` attaches it as `Authorization: Bearer ` on every Core API request. The browser never sees the token: the React tree only renders Core API responses, never the JWT itself. The HTTP client also strips auth headers from any error that reaches `Sentry.captureException`. The dashboard's `.env.local` lists no `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` or any other Core-side secret — only `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` and `CLERK_SECRET_KEY`.

steps.step: Step 1: Sign in, then in the browser dev tools confirm no `__session` JWT, no `Authorization` header on outbound requests, and no JWT in any rendered HTML.
Step 2: Tail the dev log while loading a dashboard page and confirm every Core API call carries `Authorization: Bearer ...` server-side.
Step 3: Confirm `apps/dashboard/src/lib/api/http-api-client.ts` scrubs `Authorization` from any error it forwards to Sentry.

acceptance_check.title: Stripe secrets never in dashboard env; OSS stub billing (no Stripe React/server SDK, no PaymentElement)
acceptance_check.description: AC-053 reinterprets this check for the open-source local runtime: commercial Stripe packages and PaymentElement are deliberately removed (AC-048/AC-049). The surviving security invariant is that the dashboard never holds `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`. Concretely: `@stripe/react-stripe-js`, `@stripe/stripe-js`, and `stripe` are absent from `apps/dashboard/package.json` and there are zero `@stripe` imports under `apps/dashboard/src`. `payment-modal.tsx` is an OSS stub that renders "Billing disabled in OSS build" and POSTs to `/api/billing/checkout` (Core StubBillingService → 410); checkout/portal/webhook handlers proxy to Core only. `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` is absent from `apps/dashboard/.env.example` and from any SST env injection (`sst.config.ts` removed). Scripts `setup-stripe.ts`, `delete-user.ts`, and `add-credits.ts` do not exist under `apps/dashboard/scripts/` (AC-075).

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E '@stripe|stripe-' apps/dashboard/package.json` returns zero matches; `grep -r '@stripe' apps/dashboard/src` returns zero matches; `payment-modal.tsx` contains "Billing disabled in OSS build" and no PaymentElement / loadStripe; `pnpm --filter dashboard test payment-modal` exits 0.
Step 2: Confirm `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` is NOT listed in `apps/dashboard/.env.example` and NOT referenced in `apps/dashboard/sst.config.ts` env injection (file absent in OSS).
Step 3: Confirm `apps/dashboard/scripts/setup-stripe.ts`, `apps/dashboard/scripts/delete-user.ts`, and `apps/dashboard/scripts/add-credits.ts` do not exist under `apps/dashboard/scripts/`.

acceptance_check.title: Sentry: 3 beforeSend hooks (server/edge/client) scrub PII; SDK no-ops when DSN is blank
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/instrumentation.ts` registers the server (`sentry.server.config.ts`) and edge (`sentry.edge.config.ts`) configs; `apps/dashboard/instrumentation-client.ts` registers the client config. All three `beforeSend` hooks apply identical PII scrubbing (auth headers, cookies, request bodies, Clerk tokens) for SOC2 / GDPR / LGPD compliance. `onRequestError` forwards every Next.js error to Sentry. `src/app/global-error.tsx` is the root client error boundary that also calls `Sentry.captureException` in `useEffect`. When `NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN` is blank, the SDK no-ops at init and no network call is made.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `instrumentation.ts`, `sentry.server.config.ts`, `sentry.edge.config.ts`, `instrumentation-client.ts`, and `global-error.tsx` exist and the three Sentry.*.init() calls reference the same PII scrubbing helper.
Step 2: With `NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN=` empty, load the dashboard and confirm no outbound request to `*.sentry.io`.
Step 3: Set a non-empty DSN, throw a synthetic error, and confirm the error lands in the Sentry project `causeflow-dashboard` under org `causeflow-ai`.

acceptance_check.title: SST v3: each app's sst.config.ts provisions CloudFront + WAF + Lambda + Route 53 + ACM
acceptance_check.description: `apps/website/sst.config.ts` and `apps/dashboard/sst.config.ts` each provision: a CloudFront distribution, a WAF WebACL (must live in `us-east-1` for CloudFront association — a separate `us-east-1` provider is created in each config), the Next.js compute (S3 + CloudFront + Lambda@Edge via OpenNext for the website; Lambda@Edge for the dashboard), Route 53 records, and an ACM certificate. The dashboard's config also provisions CloudWatch alarms for Lambda error rate and 5xx rate in production only. Domains: causeflow.ai / staging.causeflow.ai (website), dashboard.causeflow.ai / dashboard-staging.causeflow.ai (dashboard). The hosted zone is `Z01593322DGY9I94W9S7C`.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `apps/website/sst.config.ts` and `apps/dashboard/sst.config.ts` each declare a `us-east-1` provider for the WAF WebACL.
Step 2: Confirm both configs use `sst.aws.Nextjs` for the website and dashboard deployments.
Step 3: Confirm the dashboard config provisions CloudWatch alarms and the website config does not.

acceptance_check.title: GitHub Actions: 2 workflows (website-deploy.yml, dashboard-deploy.yml) with frozen-lockfile + type-check + lint + test + build + Docker deploy guidance
acceptance_check.description: `.github/workflows/website-deploy.yml` and `.github/workflows/dashboard-deploy.yml` are the two deploy workflows. Each sets up pnpm via `pnpm/action-setup@v4`, Node via `.nvmrc` (24), installs with `--frozen-lockfile`, then runs app-scoped `pnpm turbo check-types`, `pnpm turbo lint`, `pnpm turbo test`, and `pnpm turbo build` (with `--filter=@causeflow/{website,dashboard}...`) before deploy jobs that echo Docker Compose instructions (no `sst deploy`; see AC-050). Staging deploys trigger on push to main; production deploys trigger via `workflow_dispatch` with `inputs.stage=production`. The workflows use `environment: staging` / `environment: production` and `concurrency:` to avoid racing deploys.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm both workflow files exist under `.github/workflows/`, each referencing pnpm/action-setup@v4 and --frozen-lockfile.

Step 2: `grep -E 'sst deploy' .github/workflows/website-deploy.yml .github/workflows/dashboard-deploy.yml` returns zero matches; each workflow runs check-types, lint, test, build in sequence before the Docker deploy guidance step.
Step 3: Confirm each workflow sets `environment: staging` and `environment: production` plus a `concurrency` block.

acceptance_check.title: Composio: 15 integration types; image allow-list for logos.composio.dev and backend.composio.dev
acceptance_check.description: The dashboard's integration catalog uses Composio for third-party integration metadata. The 15 integration types (Slack, GitHub, Jira, AWS CloudWatch, HubSpot, Trello, PostgreSQL, Linear, Sentry, MongoDB, Datadog, PagerDuty, Grafana, Confluence, Webhooks) are surfaced as connection cards in the dashboard. The `Integration` domain type carries a `composioTriggerId` field. The dashboard's `next.config.mjs` `images.remotePatterns` allow-lists `logos.composio.dev` and `backend.composio.dev`. No Composio API key lives in this repo — the actual Composio connection flow runs on the Core API; the dashboard only references Composio's metadata endpoints via the image allow-list.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` lists `logos.composio.dev` and `backend.composio.dev` in `images.remotePatterns`.
Step 2: Confirm `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/integrations/domain/types.ts` defines the 15 integration types and the `Integration` type carries `composioTriggerId`.
Step 3: Confirm no `COMPOSIO_API_KEY` env var is referenced in `apps/dashboard/.env.example` or `apps/dashboard/sst.config.ts`.

acceptance_check.title: Auth.js v5 + Cognito: packages/auth/ retained for reference; only UserRole type re-export is consumed by the dashboard
acceptance_check.description: `packages/auth/` retains the legacy Auth.js v5 (`next-auth@5.0.0-beta.30`) + `@auth/core` + `@aws-sdk/client-cognito-identity-provider` wiring at `packages/auth/src/infrastructure/auth-config.ts` for historical reference. The dashboard does not import the runtime exports from this package at runtime; the only consumer is the `UserRole` type re-export (`@causeflow/auth/types`) used by `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/identity/domain/rbac/__tests__/role-guard.test.ts`. The dev credentials provider auto-enables in development only; the production build strips it.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `packages/auth/package.json` and `packages/auth/src/infrastructure/auth-config.ts` exist.
Step 2: `grep -r "from '@causeflow/auth" apps/dashboard/src` returns only the `__tests__/role-guard.test.ts` import of the `UserRole` type.
Step 3: Confirm `apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` lists `@aws-sdk/client-cognito-identity-provider` in `serverExternalPackages` so the AWS SDK stays server-side.

acceptance_check.title: AWS SDK v3 limited to tenant-provisioning-fallback.ts and delete-user.ts CLI
acceptance_check.description: The dashboard's runtime never holds AWS credentials. `@aws-sdk/client-dynamodb` and `@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb` are imported by exactly two paths: (1) `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/identity/infrastructure/tenant-provisioning-fallback.ts`, which writes a `Tenant` and `BillingAccount` directly to the Core API's DynamoDB table when the Core's `POST /v1/tenants` returns 403 — a documented temporary workaround; (2) `apps/dashboard/scripts/delete-user.ts`, which scans and batch-deletes every item in the Core's DynamoDB table scoped to a tenantId, and also cancels the corresponding Stripe customer. The provisioning fallback is called from `complete-profile-handler.ts` on a 403 from Core; the CLI script is gated by an explicit `--yes` flag. `AWS_REGION` and AWS credentials are read from the local shell env (or IAM role in prod) and are not part of the dashboard's runtime env.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -r "@aws-sdk" apps/dashboard/src` returns only the tenant-provisioning-fallback import.
Step 2: `grep -r "@aws-sdk" apps/dashboard/scripts` returns the delete-user import.
Step 3: Confirm `apps/dashboard/.env.example` does not list `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` or `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` (these come from the shell env or SSO, not the dashboard's runtime env).

acceptance_check.title: Loops.so declared as planned integration; CSP allow-list but no current consumer in this codebase
acceptance_check.description: Loops.so is declared as a planned email-marketing integration in docs and the `apps/website/.env.example` (LOOPS_API_KEY). There is no `apps/website/src/app/api/notify` route in the current codebase — `apps/website/src/app` has no `/api` directory. The only runtime reference to Loops is the `Content-Security-Policy` `connect-src` allow-list at `apps/website/next.config.mjs` (https://app.loops.so). The integration has no current consumer; it is a planned integration only.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `apps/website/src/app` has no `/api` subdirectory.
Step 2: Confirm `apps/website/next.config.mjs` lists `https://app.loops.so` in CSP connect-src.
Step 3: Confirm `LOOPS_API_KEY` is listed in `apps/website/.env.example` but no code in `apps/website/src/` imports from any loops-related module.

acceptance_check.title: Vitest 7 projects with forks pool, max 3 workers, 15s per-test timeout on the dashboard project
acceptance_check.description: The root `vitest.config.ts` defines 7 projects (shared, forms, analytics, auth, ui, website, dashboard) with a `pool: 'forks'` worker model and `poolOptions: { forks: { maxForks: 3 } }`. The dashboard project sets `testTimeout: 15000`; the other projects use the default. `pnpm vitest run` runs all 7 projects and emits a per-project pass/fail summary. `apps/dashboard/package.json` lists `vitest` as a dev dependency.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `vitest.config.ts` defines projects for shared, forms, analytics, auth, ui, website, dashboard.
Step 2: Confirm `pool: 'forks'` and `poolOptions: { forks: { maxForks: 3 } }` are set.
Step 3: Confirm the dashboard project has `testTimeout: 15000` and the others use the default.

acceptance_check.title: Playwright 4 viewports (mobile 375×812, tablet 768×1024, desktop 1280×800, wide 1440×900) chromium only, workers 3, fully parallel, trace/video/screenshot off
acceptance_check.description: `playwright.config.ts` configures Playwright 1.58.2 with `projects:` for 4 viewports (mobile 375×812, tablet 768×1024, desktop 1280×800, wide 1440×900), chromium only, `workers: 3`, `fullyParallel: true`, and `trace: 'off'`, `video: 'off'`, `screenshot: 'off'`. The `webServer` block auto-starts the production server on port 3000 if not already running. The configuration is the single Playwright config for both apps' E2E coverage.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `playwright.config.ts` declares 4 projects with the documented viewport sizes and chromium-only.
Step 2: Confirm `workers: 3`, `fullyParallel: true`, `trace: 'off'`, `video: 'off'`, `screenshot: 'off'`.
Step 3: Confirm the `webServer` block starts the production server on port 3000.

acceptance_check.title: Playwright test.beforeEach blocks analytics + tracker domains (GA4, Clarity, Intercom)
acceptance_check.description: `tests/audit.spec.ts` and `tests/visual-functional.spec.ts` declare a `test.beforeEach` that uses `page.route()` to block any request to `https://www.google-analytics.com`, `https://*.clarity.ms`, `https://clarity.ms`, and Intercom. This prevents test traffic from polluting production analytics. The block list covers both gtag's `collect` endpoint and Clarity's session-replay endpoints.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -n 'page.route' tests/audit.spec.ts tests/visual-functional.spec.ts` returns route blocks for google-analytics.com, clarity.ms, and Intercom.
Step 2: Run `pnpm exec playwright test tests/audit.spec.ts` with a console listener and confirm no request to those domains is made.
Step 3: Confirm the block list matches the documented domains in the spec (GA, Clarity, Intercom).

acceptance_check.title: pino structured JSON logs in apps/dashboard/src/lib/logger.ts; never logs raw tokens or PII
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/src/lib/logger.ts` exports a pino 10.3.1 logger configured for structured JSON output. The logger redacts auth headers, Clerk session tokens, and Stripe secrets. `withAuth` and every API handler that catches a non-recoverable error also call `dashLogger.error` with a structured payload (method, path, userId, tenantId, duration) and forward the error to Sentry. The Sentry `beforeSend` hooks apply the same redaction in the observability layer.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `apps/dashboard/src/lib/logger.ts` exports a pino logger and `apps/dashboard/package.json` lists `pino@^10.3.1` as a dependency.

Step 2: Trigger a logged error in a dashboard API route and confirm the dev log shows structured JSON with method / path / userId / tenantId, and no JWT, Authorization header, or Stripe secret in the payload.
Step 3: Confirm the Sentry beforeSend hooks in instrumentation-client.ts, sentry.server.config.ts, and sentry.edge.config.ts apply the same redaction.

acceptance_check.title: Verification protocol for dashboard changes: local Playwright with mock client → staging login via env.staging creds → aws logs tail /ecs/causeflow-staging-api --follow
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/CLAUDE.md` documents the verification protocol: (1) local sanity via `pnpm --filter dashboard dev` (port 3001) with blank `CORE_API_URL` (mock client), Playwright codegen or scripted specs, screenshots into `.artifacts/playwright/screenshots/YYYY-MM-DD_HHmm/`; (2) staging verification via the test creds in `web/apps/dashboard/.env.staging` (`STAGING_TEST_USER`, `STAGING_TEST_PASSWORD`) on `https://dashboard-staging.causeflow.ai`, with `aws logs tail /ecs/causeflow-staging-api --follow` running in parallel; (3) E2E coverage — every PR that touches dashboard UI or APIs ships a Playwright spec; (4) manual user steps (Sentry Internal Integration, GitHub OAuth app, Slack workspace approval) are surfaced in the PR description and the agent pauses for user confirmation. The `.env.staging` file holds login creds only, not runtime env.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `apps/dashboard/CLAUDE.md` documents the four-step verification protocol with the exact URL, AWS log group, and env-staging path.
Step 2: Confirm `apps/dashboard/.env.staging` exists, contains `STAGING_TEST_USER` and `STAGING_TEST_PASSWORD`, and is excluded from runtime env.

Step 3: Confirm `apps/dashboard/.gitignore` does NOT exclude `.env.staging` (Playwright reads it; the file is intentionally tracked) and the `kill_command` in the repo's CLAUDE.md kills both Next.js and Playwright processes before any test.

acceptance_check.title: `docker compose up` brings up the open-source web+core stack; website (port 3000) and dashboard (port 3001) answer 200 with no Clerk/Stripe/AWS/SST env vars configured
acceptance_check.description: From a clean monorepo root with a blank shell env (no `CLERK_*`, `STRIPE_*`, `AWS_*`, `SENTRY_*`, `LOOPS_*`, `CORE_API_URL`, or SST-injected vars set), `docker compose up -d` brings up `causeflow-postgres`, `redis`, `hindsight`, `causeflow-api`, `causeflow-worker`, `causeflow-website` (port 3000), and `causeflow-dashboard` (port 3001) per the bundled `docker-compose.yml`. Within 90 seconds `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/` returns 200 and `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3001/auth/sign-in` returns 200 (the dashboard's local sign-in form, not the Clerk-hosted UI). Grepping the dashboard container's boot log for `clerk.com`, `stripe.com`, `amazonaws.com`, `sentry.io`, or `sst.` returns zero outbound URLs.

steps.step: Step 1: With a clean shell env (`env -i PATH=$PATH`), `docker compose up -d` from the monorepo root and `docker compose ps` reports every service healthy.
Step 2: `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/` returns 200; `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3001/auth/sign-in` returns 200.
Step 3: `docker logs causeflow-dashboard 2>&1 | grep -E 'clerk\.com|stripe\.com|amazonaws\.com|sentry\.io|sst\.'` returns zero lines.

acceptance_check.title: `.env.example` is reduced to NEXT_PUBLIC_* (optional) + CORE_API_URL + JWT_SECRET; no Clerk/Stripe/AWS/Sentry/Loops/SST vars
acceptance_check.description: `apps/website/.env.example` ships with only `NEXT_PUBLIC_GA4_MEASUREMENT_ID` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_CLARITY_ID` (both optional, blank = no-op). `apps/dashboard/.env.example` ships with only `CORE_API_URL` (default `http://core-api:3099` for the docker-compose network), `JWT_SECRET` (shared with the Core container, must match the Core's `JWT_SECRET`), and the optional `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` analytics keys. `CLERK_*`, `STRIPE_*`, `AWS_*`, `SENTRY_*` (build-time), `LOOPS_*`, and any SST-injected var are absent from both files. `LOOPS_API_KEY` is removed from `apps/website/.env.example`.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E 'CLERK_|STRIPE_|AWS_|SENTRY_|LOOPS_' apps/website/.env.example apps/dashboard/.env.example` returns zero lines.
Step 2: Confirm `apps/dashboard/.env.example` lists `CORE_API_URL` and `JWT_SECRET` and `apps/website/.env.example` lists only the two `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` keys.
Step 3: Confirm the dashboard's `docker run` mounts the same `JWT_SECRET` as the Core's `docker run` (the JWT issued by the Core is verifiable by the dashboard's `verifyJwtCookie`).

acceptance_check.title: Local JWT auth replaces Clerk: sign-in form → Core `/api/v1/auth/login` → `__session` httpOnly cookie → middleware verifies → `withAuth` resolves user/tenant/role via Core `whoami`
acceptance_check.description: `apps/dashboard/src/middleware.ts` no longer imports `clerkMiddleware`. The middleware reads the `__session` httpOnly cookie (set by the dashboard's sign-in route handler), verifies it with `JWT_SECRET` using the same `jose` library the Core uses, and lets the request through. `withAuth(handler, options)` extracts the cookie, calls the Core's `GET /api/v1/whoami` with `Authorization: Bearer `, and resolves `userId` + `tenantId` + `role` from the response. The dashboard's `/auth/sign-in` and `/auth/sign-up` pages are local React forms that POST to a new dashboard route handler (`/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/register`) which proxies to the Core's endpoints, then sets the `__session` cookie. An unauthenticated `GET /dashboard` still redirects to `/auth/sign-in` (preserving AC-019's behavior).

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -r '@clerk/nextjs/server' apps/dashboard/src` returns zero matches; `grep -r 'clerkMiddleware' apps/dashboard/src` returns zero matches.
Step 2: Submit the sign-in form with a Core-issued credential and confirm the `__session` httpOnly cookie is set; reload `/dashboard` and confirm it renders without a Clerk-hosted redirect.
Step 3: `GET /dashboard` without the cookie still returns 307 to `/auth/sign-in` (AC-019's behavior is preserved).

acceptance_check.title: `@clerk/nextjs` is removed from the dashboard's `package.json`; `pnpm --filter dashboard build` succeeds with no Clerk import in the source or build output
acceptance_check.description: `@clerk/nextjs` (and any `@clerk/*` subpackage) is removed from `apps/dashboard/package.json#dependencies`. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` succeeds. `pnpm --filter dashboard build` (which runs `tsc --noEmit --project tsconfig.build.json` + `next build`) exits 0. `grep -r '@clerk' apps/dashboard/src apps/dashboard/scripts` returns zero matches. The `tsconfig.build.json` (which previously excluded Clerk type tests) is simplified to a standard dashboard build config; if any Clerk-flavored type-test files remain, they are deleted.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E '@clerk' apps/dashboard/package.json` returns zero matches.
Step 2: `pnpm --filter dashboard build` exits 0; the build log does not mention `clerk`.
Step 3: `grep -r '@clerk' apps/dashboard/src apps/dashboard/scripts` returns zero matches.

acceptance_check.title: Stub billing replaces Stripe React SDK: `payment-modal.tsx` POSTs to Core's `StubBillingService`; the plan-gate renders correctly against the stub subscription response
acceptance_check.description: `@stripe/react-stripe-js`, `@stripe/stripe-js`, and the `stripe` server SDK are removed from `apps/dashboard/package.json`. `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/billing/presentation/components/payment-modal.tsx` no longer mounts a Stripe `PaymentElement`; it renders a local "Billing disabled in OSS build" panel and POSTs to the Core's `POST /api/v1/billing/checkout-session`, which returns 410 Gone per the Core's AC-043. The plan-gate (`apps/dashboard/src/contexts/billing/application/plan-status.ts#fetchPlanStatus`) renders correctly when the Core returns `{ plan: 'free', status: 'active' }` (a fresh tenant on the OSS build). The dashboard's `POST /api/billing/checkout` and `POST /api/billing/portal` route handlers stay (they proxy to the Core) and surface the 410 from the Core as a clear "billing disabled" message.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E '@stripe|stripe-' apps/dashboard/package.json` returns zero matches; `grep -r '@stripe' apps/dashboard/src` returns zero matches.
Step 2: Open the billing modal as a fresh tenant and confirm the "Billing disabled in OSS build" panel renders (no Stripe iframe, no `*.stripe.com` request).
Step 3: `POST /api/billing/checkout` from the dashboard returns the 410 from the Core; `fetchPlanStatus()` returns `hasActivePlan: false` for a fresh tenant so the plan-gate renders.

acceptance_check.title: AWS SDK removed: `@aws-sdk/*` deps removed, `tenant-provisioning-fallback.ts` and the `setup-stripe.ts` / `delete-user.ts` CLI scripts deleted, `serverExternalPackages` block in `next.config.mjs` removed
acceptance_check.description: `@aws-sdk/client-dynamodb`, `@aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb`, and `@aws-sdk/client-cognito-identity-provider` are removed from `apps/dashboard/package.json` (dependencies) and from the root `package.json` (if present). `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/identity/infrastructure/tenant-provisioning-fallback.ts` is deleted (the OSS Core handles provisioning inside its own `register` endpoint per the Core's AC-042). `apps/dashboard/scripts/setup-stripe.ts` and `apps/dashboard/scripts/delete-user.ts` are deleted. The `serverExternalPackages: ['@aws-sdk/client-cognito-identity-provider']` block in `apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` is removed. Commercial CLIs including `add-credits.ts` are removed (AC-075). `pnpm --filter dashboard build` exits 0.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E '@aws-sdk' apps/dashboard/package.json package.json` returns zero matches; the file `tenant-provisioning-fallback.ts` does not exist; the files `setup-stripe.ts` and `delete-user.ts` do not exist under `apps/dashboard/scripts/`.
Step 2: `grep -E 'serverExternalPackages' apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` returns zero matches.
Step 3: `pnpm --filter dashboard build` exits 0; `apps/dashboard/scripts/` has no commercial billing CLIs (`add-credits.ts` removed per AC-075).

acceptance_check.title: SST removed: `sst.config.ts` files for website and dashboard are deleted; `withSentryConfig` removed; the two GitHub Actions workflows no longer run `sst deploy`; both apps run as plain Node processes inside Docker
acceptance_check.description: `apps/website/sst.config.ts` and `apps/dashboard/sst.config.ts` are deleted. The `withSentryConfig(...)` wrapper in `apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` is removed (the surrounding `next.config.mjs` keeps its `optimizePackageImports`, headers, redirects, and image allow-list). `.github/workflows/website-deploy.yml` and `.github/workflows/dashboard-deploy.yml` keep their `check-types` / `lint` / `test` / `build` steps but drop the `sst deploy` step (replaced with a comment pointing operators to the Docker build). Both apps have a multi-stage `Dockerfile` that mirrors the `relay` subproject's pattern: a builder stage that runs `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` + `pnpm build`, then a runtime stage that runs `pnpm start`. The `docker-compose.yml` from AC-044 references those Dockerfiles.

steps.step: Step 1: The files `apps/website/sst.config.ts` and `apps/dashboard/sst.config.ts` do not exist; `grep -E 'sst|withSentryConfig' apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` returns zero matches.
Step 2: `grep -E 'sst deploy' .github/workflows/website-deploy.yml .github/workflows/dashboard-deploy.yml` returns zero matches; both workflows still run `check-types` + `lint` + `test` + `build`.
Step 3: `apps/website/Dockerfile` and `apps/dashboard/Dockerfile` exist; `docker compose -f docker-compose.yml up -d` from the monorepo root brings both apps up via those Dockerfiles.

acceptance_check.title: Composio removed: `logos.composio.dev` and `backend.composio.dev` allow-list entries deleted; `composioTriggerId` field removed from the `Integration` domain type; 15 integration cards still render against the Core's stub integration endpoint
acceptance_check.description: `logos.composio.dev` and `backend.composio.dev` are removed from `next.config.mjs#images.remotePatterns` (and any CSP allow-list). The `composioTriggerId` field is removed from the `Integration` domain type at `apps/dashboard/src/contexts/integrations/domain/types.ts`; the 15 integration type identifiers (Slack, GitHub, Jira, AWS CloudWatch, HubSpot, Trello, PostgreSQL, Linear, Sentry, MongoDB, Datadog, PagerDuty, Grafana, Confluence, Webhooks) stay. The `/dashboard/integrations` page still renders 15 connection cards; the "Connect" CTA POSTs to the Core's stub integration endpoint, which returns 200 with deterministic empty data. No `COMPOSIO_API_KEY` env var is referenced anywhere in the OSS build.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E 'composio\.dev|composioTriggerId' apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs apps/dashboard/src/contexts/integrations/domain/types.ts apps/dashboard/.env.example` returns zero matches.
Step 2: Sign in as admin, load `/dashboard/integrations`, and confirm 15 integration cards are rendered (same identifiers as AC-027).
Step 3: Click a "Connect" CTA and confirm the Core's stub integration endpoint returns 200 with empty data; no `composio.dev` or `backend.composio.dev` request is made.

acceptance_check.title: Loops.so + lvh.me removed: `app.loops.so` CSP entry deleted; `lvh.me` loopback rewrite removed from the dashboard's middleware and checkout/portal handlers
acceptance_check.description: The `connect-src` CSP entry for `https://app.loops.so` is removed from `apps/website/next.config.mjs`. `LOOPS_API_KEY` is removed from `apps/website/.env.example`. The `lvh.me` → `localhost` rewrite in `apps/dashboard/src/middleware.ts` and the inverse `localhost` → `lvh.me` rewrite in the dashboard's checkout/portal API handlers are removed (no Stripe callbacks to rewrite in the OSS build). The dashboard's `curl http://localhost:3001/...` requests work as-is; no `lvh.me` host appears anywhere in the dashboard's source.

steps.step: Step 1: `grep -E 'loops\.so|app\.loops\.so' apps/website/next.config.mjs apps/website/.env.example` returns zero matches.
Step 2: `grep -rE 'lvh\.me' apps/dashboard/src apps/dashboard/next.config.mjs` returns zero matches.
Step 3: `curl -I http://localhost:3001/dashboard` in the OSS compose stack still returns 307 to `/auth/sign-in` (no lvh.me rewrite needed).

acceptance_check.title: Regression: all 43 original web acceptance checks (AC-001 through AC-043) pass against the open-source docker-compose stack
acceptance_check.description: With the OSS docker-compose stack up from AC-044, the following original checks still pass (any check that described a vendor-specific implementation now passes against the local equivalent — e.g. AC-019's redirect behavior is preserved by the local JWT middleware, AC-022's 402 CREDITS_EXHAUSTED is preserved by the local credits ledger, AC-031's "no STRIPE_SECRET_KEY in dashboard env" is trivially true because Stripe is gone, AC-037's "AWS SDK limited to two files" is trivially true because AWS SDK is gone). Concretely: `pnpm turbo check-types` + `pnpm turbo lint` + `pnpm turbo test` + `pnpm turbo build` all exit 0 inside the dashboard container; `curl http://localhost:3000/` returns 200; the dashboard's sign-in form works end-to-end with the Core's local auth; `GET /api/incidents` (with a valid cookie) returns the Core's stub data; the plan-gate renders correctly for a fresh tenant; Sentry is a no-op with `NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN` empty.

steps.step: Step 1: `docker exec causeflow-dashboard pnpm turbo check-types` and `docker exec causeflow-dashboard pnpm turbo lint` and `docker exec causeflow-dashboard pnpm turbo test` and `docker exec causeflow-dashboard pnpm turbo build` all exit 0.

Step 2: `curl http://localhost:3000/` returns 200; `curl http://localhost:3001/auth/sign-in` returns 200; submitting the sign-in form with a Core-issued credential returns a session and `GET /dashboard` renders.

Step 3: `GET /api/settings` with a valid `__session` cookie returns 200; `GET /api/billing/subscription` returns the Core's stub `{ plan: 'free', status: 'active' }`; `POST /api/incidents` decrements credits and returns 402 CREDITS_EXHAUSTED when the ledger is empty.

acceptance_check.title: OSS Playwright project: local JWT auth against compose dashboard+Core (no Clerk staging)
acceptance_check.description: A dedicated Playwright project (e.g. `dashboard-oss-e2e`) targets the OSS compose stack (dashboard on the documented compose port, `CORE_API_URL` pointing at Core `:3099`). Auth setup uses Core local register/login + session cookie only — zero Clerk, zero `.env.staging` STAGING_TEST_USER path, zero `page.route` mocks of `/api/integrations/*` or `/api/incidents*` as the pass path. `pnpm exec playwright test --project=dashboard-oss-e2e` is the command operators and harness QA run for AC-055..AC-057.

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm `playwright.config.ts` defines a `dashboard-oss-e2e` (or equivalent) project whose baseURL is the compose dashboard and whose setup uses Core local auth only.
Step 2: Confirm the project does not load Clerk auth-setup and does not depend on `STAGING_TEST_USER` / `.env.staging`.
Step 3: `pnpm exec playwright test --project=dashboard-oss-e2e --list` lists the AC-055..AC-057 specs and exits 0.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard E2E connects a Core stub connector and exercises a mock/stub upstream application
acceptance_check.description: Signed in via AC-054, the operator opens `/dashboard/integrations`, connects at least one integration through the Core OSS/stub integration API (Composio remains removed). The connect flow reaches a Core-owned mock/stub upstream application or webhook simulator (not a Playwright `page.route` fake of the dashboard BFF). After connect, the integrations UI shows the connector as connected/ready and a subsequent Core API read confirms the stub connection record exists for the tenant.

steps.step: Step 1: As local-auth admin, load `/dashboard/integrations` and complete Connect for at least one catalog card against Core stub endpoints (HTTP 200, no `composio.dev` traffic).
Step 2: Confirm a Core-owned mock/stub upstream (webhook simulator or stub app documented in Core) received the connection or a probe request attributable to that connector.
Step 3: Reload integrations and confirm the UI reflects connected state; `GET` the Core integration status for that type returns connected for the tenant.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard E2E creates a real incident that Core persists and investigates
acceptance_check.description: From the OSS dashboard (local auth), create a real incident/analysis (UI or authenticated `POST` through the dashboard BFF into Core). Core persists the incident in Postgres. The dashboard incident/analysis detail page opens a live SSE (or equivalent) feed and shows triage/investigation progress from Core — not fixture JSON injected by Playwright. Credits/ledger rules from AC-022 still apply on the OSS stack.

steps.step: Step 1: Create an incident from the dashboard against compose Core; the BFF returns success and Core `GET /api/v1/incidents/:id` (or documented equivalent) returns the same id for the tenant.
Step 2: Open the dashboard detail page and confirm a live event stream proxies Core investigation/triage events (Content-Type text/event-stream or documented equivalent).
Step 3: Within the documented timeout, the UI shows non-empty investigation progress (severity/hypothesis/agent event) sourced from Core, not from a mocked route.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard E2E proves the investigation used Ornith 9B via Core's local LLM connector
acceptance_check.description: With Ornith 9B serving on `http://127.0.0.1:8081` (`~/tools/llama-session.sh`), the AC-056 incident's triage/investigation happy path uses Core's default OpenAI-compatible LLM connector pointed at Ornith (model alias `Ornith-1.0-9B-code`). Evidence must show at least one successful completion call to the local connector during the run (Core LLM audit/metrics, llama-server access log, or documented `/admin` trace) — Anthropic must not be required and a silent DeterministicLLMClient stub must not be the pass path for this check. If Ornith is stopped, the same E2E fails closed with a clear Core health/investigation error instead of hanging or auto-passing via stub.

steps.step: Step 1: With Ornith up on :8081, run the AC-056 flow and confirm Core is configured for the OpenAI-compatible base URL `http://127.0.0.1:8081/v1` (or compose-reachable equivalent) and model `Ornith-1.0-9B-code`.
Step 2: After investigation progresses, produce evidence of ≥1 successful local LLM completion attributable to that incident (log/metric/trace).
Step 3: Stop Ornith and re-run (or probe health); Core reports LLM unavailable clearly and the E2E does not pass via DeterministicLLMClient/Anthropic fallback.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard user connects a runnable test application and enables integrations
acceptance_check.description: From the OSS dashboard (local auth), the user connects a Core-owned **runnable test application** (not a Playwright `page.route` fake) via `/dashboard/integrations`. At least two integration types (or one app plus one additional connector) are enabled against that test app through Core OSS/stub endpoints. The UI shows connected/enabled state and Core persists the connection records for the tenant. Composio remains unused.

steps.step: Step 1: Start/confirm the Core test application is reachable; from `/dashboard/integrations`, complete Connect for the test application.
Step 2: Enable at least one additional integration/connector tied to that app (or a second catalog card) through Core stub endpoints.
Step 3: Reload the page and confirm both connections remain enabled; Core API status for those integrations returns connected for the tenant.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard configures investigation LLM: Ornith default, DeepSeek V4 Flash fallback
acceptance_check.description: The OSS dashboard (or settings surface it proxies) can select Core's investigation LLM connector. Default is Ornith 9B on `:8081`. When Ornith's context window is insufficient for the investigation (documented overflow / context-too-large signal from Core), the operator switches to **DeepSeek V4 Flash** via OpenCode Go (`opencode/deepseek-v4-flash-free`) or NVIDIA NIM (`deepseek-ai/deepseek-v4-pro` or `deepseek-v4-flash`). Credentials are supplied at runtime from the operator host (`~/.config/opencode`, `~/.pi/agent/auth.json`, or env vars derived from them) and are never committed to the repo. After selection, Core health/settings reflect the active connector.

steps.step: Step 1: With Ornith up, confirm the dashboard/settings UI (or documented API) shows Ornith as the active default LLM connector.
Step 2: Simulate or trigger Ornith context-too-small / switch to DeepSeek V4 Flash (OpenCode Go or NVIDIA NIM) using runtime host credentials.
Step 3: Confirm Core reports the active connector as DeepSeek for subsequent investigation calls; no Anthropic key is required for this path.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard golden path: analyses, triage, incident chat, root cause, remediation
acceptance_check.description: Using the connected test application (AC-058) and configured LLM (AC-059), the dashboard drives a full investigation on a real Core incident: analyses list/detail update, triage assigns severity, the user can **talk to the model on the incident** (chat panel returns model-backed replies), the UI shows a **root-cause** hypothesis with evidence, and Core produces a **remediation** proposal visible in the dashboard. All progress is live from Core (SSE or documented polling), not Playwright fixtures. DeterministicLLMClient is not the pass path.

steps.step: Step 1: From the connected test app / dashboard, create or ingest an incident; open analyses/detail and confirm triage severity appears from Core.
Step 2: Use the incident chat/ask panel; receive a non-empty model-backed reply attributable to the active LLM connector.
Step 3: Confirm the UI shows a root-cause hypothesis/evidence and a remediation proposal generated for that incident before the run completes.

acceptance_check.title: Capstone OSS Playwright suite: full golden path is the delivery gate
acceptance_check.description: `pnpm exec playwright test --project=dashboard-oss-e2e` (or the documented suite name) runs one end-to-end scenario that chains AC-054..AC-060: local auth → connect test app → enable integrations → configure Ornith/DeepSeek → create incident → analyses/triage/chat/root-cause/remediation. The suite exits 0 only when every stage passes against compose Core. This suite is required evidence for Goal Review / `run_completed` on web — partial AC-055/057 smoke alone does not satisfy open-source delivery.

steps.step: Step 1: With compose Core + dashboard + test app + Ornith (or approved DeepSeek fallback) up, run the golden-path Playwright suite.
Step 2: Confirm the suite asserts each stage (connect, integrations, LLM, incident, triage, chat, root cause, remediation) with Core-backed evidence.
Step 3: Suite exits 0; artifacts/logs retained under the harness evidence path for Goal Review.

acceptance_check.title: Root compose umbrella includes platform and docs services
acceptance_check.description: At the monorepo root, `docker-compose.yml` exists and `docker compose -f docker-compose.yml config` exits 0. The rendered compose config includes the existing OSS platform services, maps website host :3000 as before, and maps the docs service from host :5181 to container :3000.

steps.step: Step 1: From the monorepo root, confirm `docker-compose.yml` exists.
Step 2: Run `docker compose -f docker-compose.yml config` and confirm it exits 0.
Step 3: Inspect the rendered config and confirm website host :3000 is still present and `causeflow-docs` maps `5181:3000`.

acceptance_check.title: Root init starts the OSS stack and prints URLs without relay
acceptance_check.description: Running `./init.sh` from the monorepo root exits 0 only after the compose stack is ready, prints a URL matrix containing http://localhost:3000, http://localhost:3001, http://localhost:3099, and http://localhost:5181 with health URLs where applicable, and does not start the relay service.

steps.step: Step 1: From the monorepo root, run `./init.sh` on a host with Docker and the Docker Compose plugin installed.
Step 2: Confirm the script waits for service readiness and exits 0 only after the stack is available.
Step 3: Confirm the printed URL matrix includes website :3000, dashboard :3001, API :3099, docs :5181, and health URLs where applicable.
Step 4: Confirm no relay container or relay startup command is launched by the root init path.

acceptance_check.title: Root README documents monorepo quickstart and required links
acceptance_check.description: The monorepo-root `README.md` exists and explains the CauseFlow monorepo, quickstart via `./init.sh`, local URLs, the published GitHub Pages docs URL, API health, links to the core, web, public-docs, and relay READMEs, and links to `docs/00-index.md`.

steps.step: Step 1: Open the monorepo-root `README.md`.
Step 2: Confirm it describes the monorepo purpose and quickstart via `./init.sh`.
Step 3: Confirm it lists local URLs, API health, the Pages docs URL, subproject README links, and `docs/00-index.md`.

acceptance_check.title: GitHub Pages workflow exports Mint docs without deploying Mintlify
acceptance_check.description: At the monorepo root, `.github/workflows/docs-pages.yml` exists, builds `public-docs` using `mint export`, and deploys the generated static artifact to GitHub Pages. The workflow does not run `mint deploy` or `mintlify deploy`.

steps.step: Step 1: Open monorepo-root `.github/workflows/docs-pages.yml`.
Step 2: Confirm the workflow builds `public-docs` with `mint export` and publishes through GitHub Pages.
Step 3: Confirm no workflow step invokes `mint deploy` or `mintlify deploy`.

acceptance_check.title: Website Docs links point to published GitHub Pages docs
acceptance_check.description: The website header, footer, shared navigation constants, and homepage main-page Docs CTA/link all point to https://vinicius91carvalho.github.io/causeflow-ai/ instead of docs.causeflow.ai, and the unit/navigation tests are updated to assert that URL.

steps.step: Step 1: Inspect website header, footer, shared navigation constants, and homepage main-page CTA/link definitions.
Step 2: Confirm every Docs link uses https://vinicius91carvalho.github.io/causeflow-ai/ and no website Docs link still targets docs.causeflow.ai.
Step 3: Run the relevant unit/navigation tests and confirm they assert the Pages URL.

acceptance_check.title: Root init fails closed for Docker, port, and compose failures
acceptance_check.description: If Docker is missing, root `./init.sh` exits non-zero with an install hint before running compose. If ports 3000, 3001, 3099, or 5181 are already in use, it exits non-zero with the explicit conflicting ports or documented reclaim instructions. If compose partially fails, it exits non-zero, prints compose `ps` and relevant logs, and does not print `Ready`.

steps.step: Step 1: Run root `./init.sh` without Docker available and confirm non-zero exit plus install hint before any compose command.
Step 2: Occupy one or more required ports among 3000, 3001, 3099, and 5181, then confirm non-zero exit with explicit port reporting or documented reclaim instructions.
Step 3: Force a compose service failure and confirm non-zero exit, compose status/log output, and no `Ready` message.

acceptance_check.title: Monorepo-root smoke preserves website and dashboard while docs are up
acceptance_check.description: AC-044 still holds from the monorepo root after the umbrella stack starts: `curl` of the website root and dashboard auth/sign-in route returns HTTP 200, and the docs site is also reachable on http://localhost:5181.

steps.step: Step 1: Start the stack from the monorepo root through the root compose/init path.
Step 2: Run `curl` against website `/` on :3000 and dashboard auth/sign-in on :3001 and confirm HTTP 200 responses.
Step 3: Run `curl` against docs on :5181 and confirm it responds successfully.

acceptance_check.title: Default OSS compose includes causeflow-test-app with correct stub upstream URL
acceptance_check.description: After starting the operator OSS stack used by the dashboard (monorepo-root and/or web compose path covered by AC-044), `causeflow-test-app` is running and Core is configured with `STUB_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL` pointing at that Docker service (not container `127.0.0.1`). A health check against the test app succeeds from the compose network.

steps.step: Step 1: Start the OSS stack via the documented root/web compose path.
Step 2: Confirm `causeflow-test-app` is listed as running in compose ps and answers health on its published/internal port.
Step 3: Confirm the API service env includes `STUB_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL` using the Docker service hostname (for example `http://causeflow-test-app:5190`), not `http://127.0.0.1:5190`.

acceptance_check.title: Integrations page connects Test Application (OSS) successfully
acceptance_check.description: On the running OSS dashboard, an authenticated user opens Integrations, sees Test Application (OSS), clicks Connect, and the flow completes without an error toast. The integration appears connected with display name Test Application (OSS).

steps.step: Step 1: Sign in to the OSS dashboard and open /dashboard/integrations.
Step 2: Locate the Test Application (OSS) card and click Connect.
Step 3: Confirm success UI (no Failed to connect toast) and a connected credential/card for Test Application (OSS).

acceptance_check.title: Test Application Test action uses Core stub probe
acceptance_check.description: After connecting Test Application (OSS), the Integrations Test control exercises Core stub probe behavior (dashboard BFF to Core `/v1/integrations/stub/probe` or equivalent stub probe path), not the AWS-oriented generic test-connection path. A healthy test app yields a successful Test result in the UI.

steps.step: Step 1: With Test Application (OSS) connected, click Test on that integration.
Step 2: Confirm the UI reports success while the test app is healthy.
Step 3: Confirm the request path is the stub probe (BFF/Core stub probe), not AWS test-connection semantics.

acceptance_check.title: Unreachable test app shows a clear Connect/Test error
acceptance_check.description: If `causeflow-test-app` is stopped or unreachable while the dashboard and API remain up, Connect or Test for Test Application (OSS) fails with a clear unreachable/error message and does not mark the integration as successfully connected/healthy.

steps.step: Step 1: Stop or block `causeflow-test-app` while leaving dashboard and API running.
Step 2: Attempt Connect or Test for Test Application (OSS).
Step 3: Confirm a clear failure message and no false healthy/connected success state.

acceptance_check.title: Dashboard billing UI and credits chrome are fully removed
acceptance_check.description: On the OSS dashboard, sidebar/nav has no Billing entry and no "investigations left" / credits remaining chrome. Navigating to /dashboard/billing redirects to the dashboard home (or equivalent non-billing landing) and never renders plan cards, quota packs, Buy more, invoices, or payment modals.

steps.step: Step 1: Open the authenticated dashboard shell and confirm Billing is absent from navigation and no credits/investigations-left widget is shown.
Step 2: Visit /dashboard/billing directly.
Step 3: Confirm redirect away from billing and that plan/quota/payment commercial UI is not rendered.

acceptance_check.title: Investigation create is not blocked by credits exhaustion
acceptance_check.description: Creating an investigation/incident through the OSS dashboard never fails with 402 CREDITS_EXHAUSTED from a local credits ledger or commercial quota gate. Operators can create investigations without purchasing credits or seeing remaining-credit limits.

steps.step: Step 1: Authenticate on the OSS dashboard with a fresh or previously "free plan" tenant.
Step 2: Create an investigation/incident through the normal UI path.
Step 3: Confirm creation succeeds without 402 CREDITS_EXHAUSTED and without a credits-exhausted blocking modal driven by a 3-credit free tier.

acceptance_check.title: Commercial Stripe/plan/credits codepaths are purged from web and Core product runtime
acceptance_check.description: The OSS product no longer ships usable Stripe SDKs, add-credits CLI, commercial billing checkout/portal clients, or credits-ledger enforcement in web or Core runtime paths required by the dashboard. Dashboard/Core billing checkout and portal endpoints are absent or return 410 Gone. Marketing paid checkout CTAs are replaced with self-host / open-source messaging (no live Stripe checkout).

steps.step: Step 1: Confirm dashboard package/runtime has no Stripe SDK dependency and `add-credits.ts` is removed.
Step 2: Call former checkout/portal (or dashboard proxies) and confirm 410 Gone or route absence; confirm Core OSS path has no Stripe client / credits reservation for investigations.
Step 3: Open marketing pricing/plan CTAs and confirm self-host/OSS messaging with no live paid checkout.

acceptance_check.title: Bookmarked billing and billing API calls stay non-commercial
acceptance_check.description: A bookmarked /dashboard/billing URL never shows "3 investigations left", plan upgrade cards, or Buy more. Any remaining dashboard billing API routes used by old clients return 410 Gone (or 404 if fully removed) rather than stub free-plan subscription payloads that reintroduce quota UX.

steps.step: Step 1: Hit /dashboard/billing as a signed-in user and confirm no investigations-left or plan-card UI.
Step 2: Request former dashboard billing subscription/checkout/portal endpoints if still routed.
Step 3: Confirm 410 Gone or 404, and that responses are not consumed to render commercial quota chrome.

decision.question: Is Clerk-staging Playwright or OSS local-auth compose Playwright the pass path for dashboard E2E?
decision.choice: OSS compose + Core local JWT auth only. Clerk staging and `.env.staging` are not the pass path for AC-054..AC-057.
decision.rationale: Open-source delivery must prove the local stack operators actually run.

decision.question: Do connector E2E tests mock the dashboard BFF or hit Core stub connectors and mock upstream apps?
decision.choice: Hit Core OSS/stub integration endpoints and Core-owned mock/stub upstream applications. Playwright `page.route` BFF mocks are not the pass path.
decision.rationale: Composio is removed; connectors must be proven through Core stubs the dashboard proxies.

decision.question: Must dashboard E2E create a real Core-persisted incident with live investigation progress?
decision.choice: Yes. Create a real incident through the dashboard into Core and assert live SSE/progress from Core, not fixture JSON.
decision.rationale: Credits, persistence, and investigation UX only count if Core actually runs the pipeline.

decision.question: What proves the investigation used Ornith 9B, and what happens when Ornith is down?
decision.choice: Default Core LLM connector is OpenAI-compatible Ornith on :8081 (model Ornith-1.0-9B-code). E2E must evidence ≥1 local completion for the incident. If Ornith is down, fail closed with a clear error — no DeterministicLLMClient/Anthropic happy-path substitute.
decision.rationale: Operator lock: local Ornith is the OSS demo LLM; silent stubs hide broken wiring.

decision.question: What is the minimum dashboard E2E that counts as open-source delivery done?
decision.choice: A single OSS Playwright golden path: connect a runnable test application, enable integrations, configure Ornith (or DeepSeek V4 Flash via OpenCode Go / NVIDIA NIM when 32k context is insufficient), create an incident, then complete analyses → triage → incident chat → root cause → remediation with live Core SSE. Tokens come from operator host config at runtime, never committed.
decision.rationale: Operator 2026-07-11: partial connector/LLM smoke is not enough; the full product loop must work.

decision.question: If Ornith 9B 32k context is too small for investigation, which LLM fallback is approved?
decision.choice: DeepSeek V4 Flash via OpenCode Go (`opencode/deepseek-v4-flash-free`) or NVIDIA NIM (`deepseek-ai/deepseek-v4-pro` / `deepseek-v4-flash` per `~/.pi/agent/models.json`), using runtime credentials from `~/.config/opencode` / `~/.pi/agent/auth.json`.
decision.rationale: Keep local Ornith as default; allow a larger-context path without Anthropic.

decision.question: What does the user connect on the dashboard for E2E — a fake route mock or a real test application?
decision.choice: A Core-owned runnable test application the user connects from `/dashboard/integrations`, which can emit alerts and answer investigation probes.
decision.rationale: Validates connectors and evidence gathering the way a real customer app would.

decision.question: Which project owns a monorepo-root OSS developer-experience goal that touches compose, docs, README, and website links?
decision.choice: The web project owns this goal and its Acceptance Checks, even though implementation may touch monorepo-root files and sibling project documentation.
decision.rationale: The visible operator experience is website/dashboard/docs entrypoints, and the existing web spec already owns OSS local-runtime acceptance.

decision.question: Which local host port should published docs use inside the umbrella compose stack?
decision.choice: Serve `causeflow-docs` on host :5181 with container :3000.
decision.rationale: Website already owns host :3000, so docs need a stable non-conflicting local port while keeping the docs container's native port.

decision.question: What public docs URL should the website use for OSS delivery?
decision.choice: Use https://vinicius91carvalho.github.io/causeflow-ai/ and replace the hardcoded docs.causeflow.ai website links.
decision.rationale: GitHub Pages is the requested open-source publishing target and matches the root docs workflow.

decision.question: Should local startup remain scattered by project, or should the monorepo root own an umbrella compose and init path?
decision.choice: Use a root `docker-compose.yml` plus a thin fail-closed root `init.sh`. Relay is excluded from root startup and remains documented in relay-specific docs only.
decision.rationale: A single root command is the simplest OSS operator path while relay remains an optional component with its own lifecycle.

decision.question: Should docs publishing use Mintlify deployment or static export through GitHub Pages?
decision.choice: The monorepo-root workflow uses `mint export` only and publishes the resulting static artifact through GitHub Pages.
decision.rationale: Static GitHub Pages publishing is auditable in the OSS repo and avoids Mintlify-hosted deployment credentials or side effects.

decision.question: How should root startup behave when Docker, ports, or compose readiness fail?
decision.choice: Fail closed: missing Docker exits with an install hint before compose; required port conflicts exit with explicit ports or documented reclaim; partial compose failure exits non-zero with `ps`/logs and never prints `Ready`.
decision.rationale: Operators should not see a successful quickstart message when the stack is unavailable or ambiguous.

decision.question: Does replacing website Docs links include only shared navigation, or also the homepage body?
decision.choice: It includes the homepage main-page Docs CTA/link in addition to header, footer, and shared navigation constants.
decision.rationale: The homepage is a primary conversion surface, so the published docs path must be discoverable outside navigation chrome.

decision.question: After Stripe removal, should OSS keep free-tier credits UI or delete billing entirely?
decision.choice: Delete billing and quota entirely from the OSS product: purge Stripe/plan/credits codepaths in web and Core, remove Billing nav and credits chrome, redirect /dashboard/billing, and stop 402 credit gates. Marketing becomes self-host/OSS oriented with no live paid checkout.
decision.rationale: Stripe is already gone; leftover "3 investigations left" and plan cards are false commercial UX. Operators need a clean self-hosted product, not a stub storefront.

decision.question: Which harness project owns a goal that changes both web and Core?
decision.choice: web owns the Acceptance Checks and Work Item catalog; Core/compose changes are required implementation for those checks. No aggregate root specification and no cross-project queue dependency.
decision.rationale: Harness requires one owning project; dashboard symptoms are the operator-visible contract.

decision.question: Should causeflow-test-app be optional or part of the default OSS compose path?
decision.choice: Always start causeflow-test-app on the default OSS compose path and set STUB_UPSTREAM_BASE_URL to the Docker service hostname.
decision.rationale: Connect currently fails when Core defaults to 127.0.0.1:5190 inside the API container; the integrations golden path requires the test app by default.

decision.question: Which Test path should Test Application (OSS) use?
decision.choice: Dashboard Test routes to Core stub probe (/v1/integrations/stub/probe), not AWS-style generic test-connection.
decision.rationale: Stub upstream health is distinct from AWS credential format checks.

decision.question: What happens when the test app is unreachable during Connect/Test?
decision.choice: Show a clear unreachable error and never mark connected/healthy success.
decision.rationale: Silent success or opaque 500s hide compose/env miswiring.

decision.question: What should old /dashboard/billing bookmarks and billing API clients see?
decision.choice: Redirect UI away from commercial billing; APIs return 410 Gone or 404; never rehydrate free-plan quota chrome like "3 investigations left".
decision.rationale: Bookmarks and stale clients must not resurrect commercial UX after the purge.
